project.name: pqforge
project.title: pqforge: Post-Quantum Application Recipes for Dart
project.tagline: Post-quantum encryption, signing, hybrid sessions, gigabyte-scale streaming, and a CLI for Dart, Flutter, and Serverpod — built on pqcrypto ML-KEM/ML-DSA.
project.summary: pqforge turns the pqcrypto ML-KEM (FIPS 203) and ML-DSA (FIPS 204) primitives into application-ready cryptography: KEM-DEM envelopes, AES-256-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD, X25519/Ed25519/ECDSA-P256 hybrids, bounded-memory gigabyte streaming, multi-recipient envelopes, Argon2id-wrapped key custody, named recipes, and a universal CLI. The core is pure Dart and web-safe — no dart:ffi.

pqcrypto_relationship.summary: pqforge is the application layer built on top of pqcrypto. pqcrypto provides the post-quantum primitives; pqforge provides everything you need to ship a feature with them.
pqcrypto_relationship.pqcrypto_is: pqcrypto is the primitives package: pure-Dart NIST FIPS 203 ML-KEM and FIPS 204 ML-DSA, plus SHA-2 and SHA-3/SHAKE, with zero runtime dependencies. It exposes raw key generation, encapsulation/decapsulation, and sign/verify over byte arrays.
pqcrypto_relationship.pqforge_is: pqforge is the composition package: KEM-DEM envelopes, AES-256-GCM/ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD, X25519/Ed25519/ECDSA-P256 hybrids, bounded-memory streaming, multi-recipient envelopes, Argon2id key custody, named recipes (files, folders, text, media, email, documents, webhooks, tokens, records, logs, artifacts), and a CLI.
differentiation: Use pqcrypto when you need the raw ML-KEM/ML-DSA algorithms and nothing else.
Use pqforge when you need to ship a feature: encrypt a file, sign a release, run a hybrid session, wrap a key, or expose a CLI.
pqforge depends on pqcrypto and never reimplements the lattice primitives; the post-quantum security claim is inherited from pqcrypto.
Both are pure Dart and web-safe; neither ships dart:ffi in its published package.
pqforge adds the classical hybrid tier (X25519, Ed25519, ECDSA-P256) and the symmetric/AEAD/KDF stack that pqcrypto deliberately leaves out.

capabilities: ML-DSA-44/65/87 detached and pre-hash signatures through pqcrypto (FIPS 204-aligned)
AES-256-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD on a pure-Dart (PointyCastle) or native (package:cryptography) engine
Bounded-memory .pqfs streaming for gigabyte-scale files: signed header plus independently authenticated frames, auto-engaged at 8 MiB
Multi-recipient envelopes: one sealed payload, the DEM key wrapped per recipient, with no wire-format change
X25519 plus ML-KEM hybrid KEM-DEM so confidentiality holds while either Module-LWE or Curve25519 stands
ML-DSA plus Ed25519 or ECDSA-P256 hybrid signatures, and standalone ECDSA-P256 (RFC 6979, low-S)
Digest-mode signing over a streamed SHA-256 for gigabyte artifacts in O(1) memory
pack/unpack a whole folder into one encrypted streaming archive (one KEM encapsulation and one signature for the tree)
Argon2id plus AES-256-GCM wrapped key custody with pluggable stores; PBKDF2 wrapping under FIPS mode
Named recipes for files, folders, text, media, email, documents, webhooks, tokens, records, logs, artifacts, and identity bindings
Universal CLI plus AOT-compiled release binaries for local, server, and release-pipeline workflows
FIPS deployment layer (PqFipsMode), swappable lattice backend (PqLatticeProvider), and a swappable DRBG (PqRandom.generator)
Pure-Dart, web-safe core (no dart:ffi); dart:io file streaming behind package:pqforge/pqforge_io.dart
does_not_provide: A hard constant-time guarantee for Dart runtimes
Public-key trust, identity vetting, or legal e-signature policy
Replay stores, authorization policy, TLS, or secure infrastructure operations

allowed_claims: Application-layer composition helpers for KEM-DEM, AEAD sessions, hybrid key agreement, streaming, multi-recipient envelopes, wrapped key custody, signatures, recipes, and CLI workflows.
evidence_boundary.summary: The package provides application composition around pqcrypto evidence. It is not a validated cryptographic module.

profiles.name: compact
profiles.use: smaller demos, constrained payloads, and low-bandwidth signatures

profiles.name: maximum
profiles.use: long-lived records, archives, media, and high-value artifacts

recipes.name: Local File Vault
recipes.apps: developer machines, backup jobs, admin laptops, server batch jobs
recipes.caveat: Use wrapped secret keys and external storage policy.

recipes.name: Folder Archive
recipes.apps: case files, release bundles, evidence folders, internal archives
recipes.caveat: Each file is an independent envelope; folder commands run files concurrently via --concurrency.

recipes.name: Folder Pack Archive
recipes.apps: many small files, site exports, evidence bundles, dataset shards
recipes.caveat: One KEM encapsulation and one signature for the whole tree; restores path-traversal-safe and streams end to end.

recipes.name: Large Files And Media Streaming
recipes.apps: gigabyte media, datasets, disk images, backups, VM snapshots
recipes.caveat: Streaming I/O lives behind the dart:io entrypoint; the working set is roughly two frames regardless of file size.

recipes.name: Multi-Recipient Envelopes
recipes.apps: shared vaults, team archives, escrow, break-glass recipients
recipes.caveat: The payload is sealed once and key-wrapped per recipient; the app still authenticates each recipient public key.

recipes.name: Text Snippets
recipes.apps: notes, secrets, prompts, policy snippets, short messages
recipes.caveat: UTF-8 text only; binary data should use file or media recipes.

recipes.name: Media Assets
recipes.apps: photos, audio, video, PDFs, campaign media, identity images
recipes.caveat: Bind MIME type and media id; classify content before storing.

recipes.name: Document Signing
recipes.apps: contracts, reports, certificates, approvals, policy documents
recipes.caveat: Application owns legal policy and signer identity vetting.

recipes.name: Hybrid And Classical Signatures
recipes.apps: release artifacts, firmware, long-lived approvals, dual-trust signing
recipes.caveat: require-both vs accept-either is the app's policy; --digest signs the streamed SHA-256 for gigabyte inputs.

recipes.name: Webhook Integrity
recipes.apps: payment callbacks, audit delivery, server-to-server events
recipes.caveat: Application owns replay windows and key publication.

recipes.name: Signed Tokens
recipes.apps: API capabilities, admin actions, short-lived grants
recipes.caveat: Application owns revocation and authorization policy.

recipes.name: Private Email Payloads
recipes.apps: encrypted mail bodies, secure notifications, outbound archive mail
recipes.caveat: Transport headers and metadata may remain visible unless the app hides them.

recipes.name: Government And Medical Records
recipes.apps: public-sector records, patient records, registry payloads
recipes.caveat: Application owns access control, retention, and audit policy.

recipes.name: Software Artifacts
recipes.apps: release bundles, firmware, packages, configuration rollouts
recipes.caveat: Sign version and target metadata to block rollback replay.

recipes.name: Hybrid Server Sessions
recipes.apps: Serverpod, API services, local agents, backend handshakes
recipes.caveat: Authenticate public key bundles and design replay/session storage.

faq.question: What is pqforge?
faq.answer: pqforge is a pure-Dart post-quantum application toolkit. It turns the ML-KEM and ML-DSA primitives from pqcrypto into encrypted files, folders, text, media, email payloads, records, signed documents, signed webhooks, signed tokens, release artifacts, tamper-evident logs, hybrid sessions, wrapped key custody, and a universal CLI.

faq.question: How is pqforge different from pqcrypto?
faq.answer: pqcrypto is the primitives layer: FIPS 203 ML-KEM, FIPS 204 ML-DSA, SHA-2, and SHA-3/SHAKE with zero runtime dependencies. pqforge is the application layer built on top of it: KEM-DEM envelopes, AES-256-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD, X25519/Ed25519/ECDSA-P256 hybrids, key custody, streaming, multi-recipient envelopes, named recipes, and a CLI. Use pqcrypto for raw algorithms; use pqforge to ship a feature.

faq.question: Is pqforge post-quantum / quantum-safe?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses NIST FIPS 203 ML-KEM for key establishment and FIPS 204 ML-DSA for signatures through pqcrypto, so confidentiality and signatures resist quantum attacks. Hybrid modes add X25519, Ed25519, or ECDSA-P256 so security also holds if a classical break ever lands on the post-quantum scheme.

faq.question: Does pqforge work on Flutter and the web?
faq.answer: Yes. The core import, package:pqforge/pqforge.dart, is pure Dart and web-safe with no dart:ffi, so the same code runs on the Dart VM, Flutter, and the web. Gigabyte-scale dart:io file streaming lives behind package:pqforge/pqforge_io.dart. On Flutter, FlutterCryptography.enable() adds hardware-backed AEAD.

faq.question: Can pqforge encrypt gigabyte files without running out of memory?
faq.answer: Yes. Inputs at or above 8 MiB automatically use the .pqfs streaming container: a signed master header followed by independently authenticated frames, with a working set of roughly two frames regardless of total size. Per-frame sequence and final-flag binding prevent truncation, reordering, duplication, and splicing.

faq.question: Can one ciphertext target multiple recipients?
faq.answer: Yes. Repeat --recipient-public on the encrypt commands (or use PqMultiRecipient in the library). The payload is sealed exactly once and the DEM key is wrapped to each additional recipient as a metadata entry, costing about 1.6 KB and 2 ms per extra recipient instead of a full re-encryption. Entries can individually be hybrid, and it works for both one-shot envelopes and .pqfs streams.

faq.question: Which ciphers does pqforge support, and which is fastest?
faq.answer: AES-256-GCM (default) and ChaCha20-Poly1305, on a pure-Dart PointyCastle engine or the native package:cryptography engine. Where hardware AES is not dispatched, ChaCha20-Poly1305 measures about 30.4 MiB/s versus 11.5 MiB/s for AES-256-GCM (about 2.6x); with hardware AEAD both exceed 1 GB/s. RC4 is rejected.

faq.question: What is the difference between the cryptography and pure-dart engines?
faq.answer: --engine cryptography (the default) uses package:cryptography and is roughly 10x faster than the PointyCastle reference even in pure Dart, and hardware-backed on Flutter. --engine pure-dart uses PointyCastle only. The wire formats are engine-independent, so an envelope sealed by one engine opens on the other.

faq.question: Is pqforge FIPS validated?
faq.answer: No. pqforge composes FIPS 203/204-aligned primitives from pqcrypto but is not a CMVP or FIPS 140 validated cryptographic module. A PqFipsMode deployment layer restricts suites to AES-256-GCM and PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 key wrapping (SP 800-132) and allows a swappable DRBG for validated-module deployments.

faq.question: Can pqforge sign and verify documents and releases?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides ML-DSA detached signatures, recipe-bound signatures for documents, text, media, artifacts, tokens, and webhooks, hybrid ML-DSA + Ed25519/ECDSA-P256 signatures, and standalone ECDSA-P256 (RFC 6979 deterministic nonces, canonical low-S). AES never signs.

faq.question: Can I sign a gigabyte artifact without reading it all into memory?
faq.answer: Yes. hybrid-sign --digest and ecdsa-sign --digest sign the streamed SHA-256 of the input under a domain-separation label, recorded in the signature JSON so the verify commands re-hash automatically. Envelope signatures are likewise computed over a header-plus-payload hash, so signing cost does not scale with payload size.

faq.question: Can pqforge bundle a whole folder into one file?
faq.answer: Yes. pqforge pack collapses a whole folder into one encrypted streaming archive with a single KEM encapsulation and signature for the tree, roughly 100x less ciphertext overhead than per-file envelopes for a thousand small files. pqforge unpack restores it path-traversal-safe, and a failed unpack removes everything it created.

faq.question: What does hybrid mean in pqforge?
faq.answer: Two independent hard problems guard the same secret. Hybrid encryption combines the ML-KEM shared secret with an ephemeral X25519 exchange using the IETF concatenate-then-KDF construction, so confidentiality holds while either Module-LWE or Curve25519 stands. Hybrid signatures pair an ML-DSA signature with an Ed25519 or ECDSA-P256 signature over the same message.

faq.question: Where does the CLI store keys?
faq.answer: In the directory passed to keygen --out-dir. Public keys are raw JSON. Secret keys are wrapped JSON when a passphrase source is supplied. keygen emits the full hybrid keyset by default: ML-KEM and ML-DSA plus X25519, Ed25519, and ECDSA-P256.

faq.question: Are CLI keys password protected?
faq.answer: Yes when keygen is run with --passphrase-env, --passphrase-file, or --passphrase. Wrapped secrets use Argon2id for passphrase-to-key derivation and AES-256-GCM for authenticated encryption of the key bytes, with AAD binding the key kind, algorithm, and key id.

faq.question: Can I inspect an encrypted file without decrypting it?
faq.answer: Yes. pqforge inspect describes any .pqf, .pqfs, key, or signature file: format, profile, suite (for example ML-KEM-1024 + X25519 then HKDF-SHA-512 then AES-256-GCM), engine, signature, recipients, and metadata, without needing a secret key.

faq.question: Does pqforge provide identity, trust, or replay protection?
faq.answer: No. pqforge composes cryptographic building blocks. Public-key trust, identity vetting, replay windows, authorization policy, session storage, and TLS remain the application's responsibility. A self-provided public key is not an identity.

faq.question: Is the published package pure Dart? Does it use FFI?
faq.answer: Yes, it is pure Dart with no dart:ffi, so it runs everywhere Dart runs, including the web. The OpenSSL interop harness used in CI to prove byte-compatibility with the system libcrypto is a separate publish_to: none dev package and is never shipped.

faq.question: Does pqforge support RC4?
faq.answer: No. RC4 is not post-quantum, not authenticated encryption, not in pqcrypto, and not safe for new systems. pqforge offers AES-256-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 instead.

faq.question: Can AES sign documents?
faq.answer: No. AES encrypts; ML-DSA, hybrid signatures, or ECDSA-P256 sign and verify. Encryption and signing are distinct operations with distinct keys.

faq.question: Which apps can use pqforge?
faq.answer: Local file vaults, folder and pack archives, server export jobs, gigabyte media and dataset pipelines, multi-recipient shared vaults, webhook systems, token issuers, document workflows, email payload protection, government and medical record systems, signed release and firmware pipelines, tamper-evident logs, and hybrid Serverpod/API server sessions.
